text: The laparoscopic camera reveals the right iliac fossa, showing significant inflammation in the area.
The surgeon visually examines the appendix, noting that the base appears relatively intact while the tip and body are visibly inflamed.
The appendix is visibly adhered to the small intestine, forming a lump due to inflammation.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of phlegmon in the right iliac fossa, indicating inflammation and tissue swelling around the appendix.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to separate the edematous lump from the small intestine, revealing the appendix on the left side and the small intestine on the right side within the inflamed surgical field.
The surgeon observes a fecolith within the appendix, which appears swollen and completely thrombosed, indicating significant inflammation.
The surgeon has successfully separated the appendix from the small intestine, which was previously adhered and formed a lump.
The surgeon uses a semi-traumatic grasper to hold the appendix, lifting it upwards and approaching its base, with visible phlegmon surrounding the area.
At the base of the appendix, there are visible adhesions and inflammation, indicating a challenging dissection area.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the major appendix, which is visibly swollen and thrombosed, from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies anterometrial traction to the appendix, lifting it to improve visibility and access to the base for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the major appendix, visibly cutting through the tissue with precision.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the base of the appendix, with the tinea coli visible nearby.
The surgeon identifies phlegmons formed near the appendix, with the ileocecal junction visible below these structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to fully skeletonize the base of the appendix, carefully exposing and separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon ties a knot near the tinea coli, separating the mesoappendix at its base. The dissection continues on the opposite side, carefully isolating the appendicular artery within the lump of tissue.
The surgeon positions the Teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the appendix to prevent vibrations from damaging it, and carefully separates the major appendix from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply intromedial traction while the right hand completes the separation, revealing the base of the appendix.
The base of the appendix is clearly identified. The surgeon prepares to apply a simple extracorporeal Misrash knot around the base, ensuring secure ligation.
The surgeon lifts the inflamed appendix, revealing that the base of the appendix appears normal and unaffected.
The surgeon applies a misrash knot to secure the base of the appendix, using surgical instruments to hold the knot in place.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to bring the tip to the base of the appendix near the C curve, applying traction to ligate the base. A dumbbell-shaped knot is formed, securing the appendix at the junction.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut the appendix, followed by the harmonic scalpel to transect the remaining appendix from the base. The harmonic scalpel burns the mucosa, disinfecting fecal matter and leaving a 5mm stump.
The surgeon uses the tip of the harmonic scalpel to cauterize the remaining mucosa of the appendix stump, ensuring complete tissue burn and closure.
The surgeon uses suction to remove minimal fluid and slight bleeding from the fossa, followed by irrigation. The appendix is visible outside the surgical field.
The surgeon injects 5 ml of betadine over the surgical site, ensuring thorough antiseptic coverage.
The surgeon uses suction to remove any remaining inflammation or infection from the surgical site, ensuring that no betadine solution is left in the area.
The surgeon replaces the ten mm telescope with a five mm telescope, inserting it into the left iliac fossa. A high-quality endo bag is used for specimen retrieval.
The surgeon uses an endo catch device from Covidien, featuring a metal ring and self-retaining endo bag, to secure the appendix within the bag.
The surgeon uses a 5mm telescope to visualize the appendix, which is extracted through the umbilical port using Oham forceps. The mouth of the appendix is opened outside the body for removal.
